title: Tantalum Damascene Coplanar Waveguide Resonators Fabricated Using 300 mm Scale Processes
authors: Ekta Bhatia, Yingge Du, Krishna P Koirala, Chung Kow, Mingzhao Liu, Juan Macy, Tharanga R. Nanayakkara, Francisco Ponce, Satyavolu S. Papa Rao, Drew J. Rebar, Peter V. Sushko, Brent A VanDevender, Chongmin Wang, Marvin G. Warner, Zhihao Xiao
abstract: Surface oxides contribute to losses in superconducting transmon devices resulting in degraded performance. We explore the use of the damascene process to replace the sidewall native oxide of a device with a metal/substrate interface. We simulate sidewall oxidation by burying an oxide layer during fabrication. We observe a modest improvement between the two types of devices, which is suggestive of a reduction in the surface participation ratio.
summary: This paper investigates a new fabrication method called the damascene process to improve the quality of superconducting tantalum resonators. By embedding the metal within the substrate, the researchers replaced lossy metal/air sidewall interfaces with metal/substrate interfaces. The study demonstrates that avoiding native oxide formation at these interfaces can significantly increase the resonator's quality factor. This approach is highly scalable, utilizing 300 mm industrial-scale fabrication processes, which is crucial for the future of large-scale quantum computing hardware.
main_problem: Surface oxides on the sidewalls of superconducting tantalum devices contribute to dielectric losses, degrading the performance of transmon qubits.
main_result: The damascene fabrication process successfully replaces the metal/air interface with a metal/substrate interface, leading to a statistical improvement in quality factor of approximately 2x for pristine interfaces compared to buried oxide interfaces.
method: The researchers used a 300 mm scale damascene process involving trench etching, Ta deposition, and chemical mechanical planarization (CMP), followed by cryogenic characterization and STEM/EDS analysis.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of Tantalum (Ta) based superconducting coplanar waveguide (CPW) resonators fabricated on 300 mm Si (100) wafers, including both capacitive and inductive coupling configurations.
key_observables: Resonance frequencies, coupling quality factor (Qc), internal quality factor (Qi), kinetic inductance (Lki), and electric field distribution profiles.
important_parameters: Temperature range of 25 mK to 350 mK, power levels from -40 to -80 dBm, and 300 mm wafer scale.
main_assumptions: The numerical simulations neglected kinetic inductance initially, requiring a post-hoc correction to match experimental resonance frequencies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the damascene fabrication process flow; Figure 2 displays SEM images of the resonator structures; Figure 3 illustrates simulated electric field distributions; Figure 12 shows Qi as a function of temperature; Figure 13 compares quality factors across different device types.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of surface oxides, describes the damascene fabrication process and experimental setup, presents numerical simulations and characterization results (STEM/EDS), discusses the performance of different device variants, and concludes with an analysis of loss mechanisms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Damascene process flow for fabricating tantalum-based su- perconducting resonators. (i–iii) Pattern definition and anisotropic Si etching using a SiO2/SiN hard mask. (iv) Deposition of a 3 nm ALD TaN seed layer. (v) Ta sputter deposition to fill the etched Si features. (vi) CMP removal of Ta from field regions, leaving α-Ta in the trenches. (vii) Post-CMP oxide removal exposing the final recessed Ta structures. A-E show the five device variants studied, distinguished by buried-oxide vs. pristine interfaces and different surface-treatment/capping schemes. (Dimensions not to scale)

FIG. 2. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images depicting an example/structure of an inductively (left) and capacitively (right) coupled resonator. The holes in the ground plane are a requirement for the CMP process to maintain 50% metal coverage.

FIG. 3. Electric field distribution profiles of the quarter-wave reso- nance modes of the 4 CPW resonator along the common feed line. The actual device pattern is placed to the background to help visual- ization.

FIG. 4. (A) STEM image of a Device A type showing clearly defined features relative to the Si substrate. (B) Composite elemental anal- ysis of the device showing the N, O, Si, and Ta. Due to the overlap of the Si-K and Ta-M lines the Ta region is tinted from limitation on elemental identification. Enhanced image of the boxed area showing (C) the trapped oxygen and (D) N layer at the Si interface. The ∼3 nm of TaN exposed to atmosphere (O from TaON) is clearly visible.

FIG. 8. (A) STEM image of a Device E type showing clearly defined features relative to the Si substrate. (B) Composite elemental anal- ysis of the device showing the N, O, Si, and Ta. Due to the overlap of the Si-K and Ta-M lines the Ta region is tinted from limitation on elemental identification. (C) Oxygen elemental analysis indicates no oxide at the Si/Ta interfaces. The 2 nm of PVD is present but appears diffused.

FIG. 5. (A) STEM image of a Device B type showing clearly defined features relative to the Si substrate. (B) Composite elemental anal- ysis of the device showing the N, O, Si, and Ta. Due to the overlap of the Si-K and Ta-M lines the Ta region is tinted from limitation on elemental identification. Enhanced image of the boxed area showing (C) the trapped oxygen and (D) N layer at the Si interface. The ∼3 nm of TaN exposed to atmosphere (O from TaON) is also visible.

FIG. 6. (A) STEM image of a Device C type showing clearly defined features relative to the Si substrate. (B) Composite elemental anal- ysis of the device showing the N, O, Si, and Ta. Due to the overlap of the Si-K and Ta-M lines the Ta region is tinted from limitation on elemental identification. (C) Oxygen elemental analysis indicates no oxide at the Si/Ta interfaces. The 1 nm of TaN is not visible.

FIG. 7. (A) STEM image of a Device D type showing clearly defined features relative to the Si substrate. (B) Composite elemental anal- ysis of the device showing the N, O, Si, and Ta. Due to the overlap of the Si-K and Ta-M lines the Ta region is tinted from limitation on elemental identification. (C) Oxygen elemental analysis indicates no oxide at the Si/Ta interfaces. The 6 nm of TaN is clearly visible.

FIG. 10. (Top) Raw data from VNA for both narrow and wide band scans. The electrical delay is fitted to the wide band data excluding the middle region (×3 width of narrow band). (Bottom) The phase model is fitted to the background corrected data. The thick gold curve indicates the region where the fit is performed, while the narrow gold curve shows the extrapolation of the fit to the wide-band data, with good agreement observed.

FIG. 9. Full scan of (top) magnitude and (bottom) phase of S21. The drop in gain between 3 and 4 GHz is from the cryogenic circulator. Insets show enhanced image around a single resonator for Device C.
